versionNotes: Sprint 4 W13 Goal Statement v2 — restructured to 4 required + 2 optional with AI sparring on new-goal and why-and-done (merged). Worked examples in a leading inspirational instructional block. Former rejection warning reframed as growth prompt. Confirmations removed.
title: Sprint 4 Goal Statement
description: ~30 minutes. You leave with a Sprint 4 goal that actually shapes the next 4 weeks — and a concrete definition of done that makes your final defense easier to write. Spend time here; it saves rework later.

questions.sectionTitle: Before you start — what strong Sprint 4 goals sound like
questions.infoBlock:
Read this first · 60 seconds

Is this goal going to help you grow? You’ll get feedback to sharpen it if the goal is too vague or “done” isn’t concrete. A strong Sprint 4 goal is specific, grounded in Sprint 3, and has an artifact you could hand someone at the end.

See what strong Sprint 4 goals sound like below:

Strong · solo

“Extend my Sprint 3 MDRO decontamination protocol with a retry-and-logging layer for failures. By end of Sprint 4 I’ll have a working retry implementation, a log of 5 real failures it caught, and a 1-page post-mortem analyzing patterns.”

Strong · pair-merge

“[Partner] and I are merging our MDRO stakeholder-comms tools into one unified dashboard. By end of Sprint 4 we’ll have a single dashboard with both of our stakeholder workflows, a demo recording showing a real use case, and a written rationale for which of our original design choices we kept and which we dropped.”

Too vague

“Keep improving on my Sprint 3 work and learning more about MDRO systems.” — not concrete, no done state.
questions.prompt: Read the examples above, then start your draft below. You can always come back to these.

questions.infoBlock:
Field 1 of 4 required · Choose your path

How are you approaching Sprint 4? Pick one of the two paths below and type it as your answer.

Solo continue

I'm staying with my current problem and building deeper on my Sprint 3 work.

Pair-merge

I'm combining forces with a classmate to tackle a shared problem together.

If you want to switch to an entirely new problem, talk to the instructor first. That path exists but needs a conversation.
questions.prompt: Type either "Solo continue" or "Pair-merge" — exactly as written above.

questions.infoBlock:
Optional · If naming it sharpens your thinking

What did you ship or demonstrate in Sprint 3?

You just finished Sprint 3, so you already know. Answer only if writing it down helps you ground your new goal. Link to your Solution Brief if helpful: S3: Solution Brief
questions.prompt: What did you ship or demonstrate in Sprint 3?
questions.placeholder: Describe your Sprint 3 deliverable and what state it's in now...

questions.infoBlock:
Optional · Depth, not core

What did you learn about your problem, your approach, or yourself as a builder that changes how you’ll work in Sprint 4?
questions.prompt: What did Sprint 3 teach you that changes how you'll work in Sprint 4?
questions.placeholder: Be specific about what shifted in your understanding...

questions.infoBlock:
Field 2 of 4 required · The goal

What is the specific, incremental goal you’re setting for Sprint 4?

This should build meaningfully on Sprint 3 — not repeat it, not ignore it.
questions.prompt: State your specific, incremental goal for Sprint 4.
questions.placeholder: State your goal clearly enough that someone else could tell whether you achieved it...
questions.sparringRole: Is this goal specific enough that someone else could tell if the student achieved it? Push on 'what's the first concrete thing you'd do on Monday?' If the goal is too vague or too ambitious for 4 weeks, help the student shrink and sharpen it. If it's concrete and scoped, affirm it and ask what evidence they'll capture along the way.

questions.sectionTitle: 3. Why this goal, and what will exist that doesn't now
questions.infoBlock:
Field 3 of 4 required · Why + Done

Why is this the right next step for you, and what concrete artifact or evidence will exist at the end of Sprint 4 that doesn’t exist today?

Think in terms of artifacts: a demo, a piece of evidence, a working prototype, a written argument. “Better understanding” is not done — “a 2-page brief defending my design choice” is.
questions.prompt: Why is this the right next step, and what concrete artifact or evidence will exist at the end of Sprint 4 that doesn't exist today?
questions.placeholder: The why in one or two sentences, then the concrete 'done' artifact...
questions.sparringRole: Can the student name what 'done' looks like concretely enough that someone else could verify it? If they said 'I'll understand X better,' that's not done — that's intention. Push them toward an artifact (demo, brief, log, prototype, written rationale, dataset) that exists at the end of Sprint 4. If they've named something concrete, affirm it and ask how they'll show it in their defense.

questions.sectionTitle: 4. Pair-merge details — required if you chose Pair-merge
questions.infoBlock:
Optional · but required if you chose Pair-merge

Only if you chose Pair-merge above — in that case this is required for your own clarity. Otherwise skip.

Who is your partner, what does the merge look like, and how are you combining your Sprint 3 work?
questions.prompt: If Pair-merge: who is your partner, and how are you combining your Sprint 3 work?
questions.placeholder: Name your partner and describe how the two projects connect... (skip entirely if Solo continue)
